title: Kalawa, Memory, and Home
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: Every year during Tihar, my mother would tie kalawa around my wrist after prayers. I did not think of it as political then. I thought of it as ordinary.
content: Every year during Tihar, my mother would tie kalawa around my wrist after prayers. I did not think of it as political then. I thought of it as ordinary — the smell of incense, the sound of mantras, everyone together. Moving to the United States made those ordinary things feel heavy with meaning. When I look at kalawa now, I see memory, migration, and responsibility braided together. This course helped me name what I was already living: that home is not a place you leave but something you carry, and that the stories we inherit come with questions we are obligated to ask.

title: Learning to Become a Good Relative
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: Krawec writes that becoming kin is not a feeling. It is a practice. I did not understand what that meant until I started asking whose land I was studying on.
content: Krawec writes that becoming kin is not a feeling. It is a practice. I did not understand what that meant until I started asking whose land I was studying on, whose history had been erased to make space for the version I was taught. Growing up in Nepal, I learned that relationships come with responsibilities — to family, to neighbors, and to community. Krawec expanded that circle. Becoming a good relative means learning the histories that were hidden from you and asking what those histories demand of you now.

title: What Feminism Found in Me
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: Sara Ahmed says feminism is how we pick each other up. I found feminism first not in a book but in watching my mother carry everything without anyone naming what she was carrying.
content: Sara Ahmed says feminism is how we pick each other up. I found feminism first not in a book but in watching my mother carry everything without anyone naming what she was carrying. The labor was real. The invisibility was also real. This course gave me language for both. It also asked me something harder: not just to name what I noticed, but to ask what I am willing to do about it. Feminist homework, Ahmed calls it. I am still working through the assignment.

title: Leaving Medicine Behind
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: In Nepal, medicine was the dream everyone understood. It was respected, secure, and familiar. What surprised me was realizing it was not my dream.
content: In Nepal, medicine was the dream everyone understood. It was respected, secure, and familiar. My father once wanted to become a doctor but could not because of financial limitations, and I grew up carrying that dream as if it belonged to me. When I scored highly on the medical entrance exam, I expected relief. Instead, I felt uncertain. Choosing mathematics meant questioning expectations that came from family, culture, and class. Looking back, that decision taught me something about social location. The paths available to us are shaped by histories and pressures that often feel invisible until we try to step away from them.

title: Across Difference
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: When I arrived in the United States, I expected my differences to separate me from others. Instead, many of my most meaningful conversations began with those differences.
content: When I arrived in the United States, I expected my differences to separate me from others. Instead, many of my most meaningful conversations began with those differences. Through interfaith programs, I sat with people from different religions, cultures, and worldviews. We talked about family, identity, grief, belonging, and hope. Nobody was trying to win an argument. We were trying to understand one another. Krawec's idea of becoming good relatives helped me make sense of those experiences. Being a good relative is not about sameness. It is about responsibility, curiosity, and care across difference.

title: Migration and Belonging
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: I used to think belonging came from fitting in. Living between Nepal and the United States taught me something different.
content: I used to think belonging came from fitting in. Living between Nepal and the United States taught me something different. The longer I stayed away from home, the more I realized belonging is not about becoming someone else. It is about finding spaces where you can bring your whole self. My language, religion, memories, and experiences do not disappear when I cross borders. They travel with me. This course challenged me to think about belonging not as acceptance through sameness but as a culture built through relationships, care, and mutual recognition.

title: The Stories My Grandparents Carried
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: Some of the most important lessons I learned were never written down. They were told across dinner tables and village paths.
content: Some of the most important lessons I learned were never written down. They came from stories told by my grandparents. Stories about hardship, migration, community, and survival. Before this course, I rarely thought of those stories as knowledge. I saw them as family history. Decolonial feminism pushed me to reconsider that assumption. It asks whose knowledge is treated as legitimate and whose is ignored. My grandparents may never have written books, but they taught me values, responsibilities, and ways of understanding the world that continue to shape who I am.

title: Feminist Homework
author: Bhushan Sah
preview: I used to think learning happened in classrooms. Ahmed's idea of feminist homework made me realize learning often begins after class ends.
content: I used to think learning happened in classrooms. Ahmed's idea of feminist homework made me realize learning often begins after class ends. Homework is not just reading a chapter or writing an essay. It is noticing who speaks and who remains silent. It is questioning assumptions that once felt normal. It is reflecting on how our actions affect others. The more I think about feminist homework, the more I see it in ordinary moments. The assignment is never really finished. It follows us into our relationships, communities, and everyday decisions.
